title: Oil & the State: NOCs, Petrostates & Energy Security
quick_definition: The listed Western majors are a minority of world oil. Most of it belongs to national oil companies — state-owned producers — sitting inside petrostates whose budgets, and whose foreign policy, run on the barrel.
explainer_covers: The ownership reality. NOCs — Saudi Aramco, ADNOC, Petrobras, PetroChina, and peers — control roughly 70% of the world's reserves and production (★G312). The investable GICS sector floats on top of a state-dominated base, which is why the majors are price-takers: the marginal supply decision often sits with governments, not markets (the tie back to Z3.3 — OPEC is NOCs coordinating).

The petrostate & the fiscal breakeven. In a petrostate (Saudi, Russia, Nigeria, Venezuela) oil is the fiscal core; each has a fiscal-breakeven oil price it needs to fund its budget — which drives its OPEC behavior. Petrostate budget math is upstream of the price the whole sector earns.

Energy security (Yergin). Oil is strategic, not merely economic: sanctions, embargoes, and supply weaponization move it. The 2022 Russia–Ukraine rupture repriced European gas overnight and is the modern exhibit of oil-and-gas-as-statecraft.

The resource curse & petrodollars. Oil wealth can hollow out institutions and other industries (the resource curse); and oil revenue recycled through global markets (petrodollars, The World for Sale) is a macro force in its own right.

The Aramco footnote. Aramco's IPO valued the largest NOC near $2 trillion (★G53/★G102) — the biggest listing ever and a partial privatization that made the state-oil model briefly a public-market object.

connects_to.note: sovereign risk — the petrostate as issuer
the cast — NOCs named up front

connects_to_raw: Z3.3 (OPEC — NOCs coordinating supply) · `macro-economics.z5.3` (current accounts — petrostate external balances) · `macro-economics.z5.4` (sovereign risk — the petrostate as issuer) · ★G53 / ★G102 (the Aramco valuation footnote) · Z1.1 (the cast — NOCs named up front).
real_world_layer: Yergin Oil and the State / sanctions chapters; Aramco IPO; petrostate fiscal breakevens.

title: The Energy Transition: The Sector Prices Its Own Sunset
quick_definition: Energy is the one sector whose core product may peak and decline. The energy transition — the shift toward lower-carbon energy — poses a demand question no other sector faces, and turns some booked reserves into potentially stranded assets.
explainer_covers: The transition, defined. The multi-decade shift from hydrocarbons toward electrification and lower-carbon sources, driven by EV adoption (eating gasoline demand), policy & carbon pricing, and falling renewables cost (Yergin, Ch. 41–45) — ★G313.

Stranded assets — the balance-sheet contradiction. Reserves are booked as assets (Z2.1/★G305), but if demand falls or policy tightens, some may become unburnable or uneconomic — never produced. The sector's own books carry an asset the transition says might not be one: the direct internal tension with ★G305, and the supply-side answer to the demand-side repricing GAPs already flagged in real-estate.z5.6/z3.10.

The sector's responses. Carbon capture (CCS), renewable diesel (Z2.3's Valero pivot), hydrogen, and the services pivot (Z2.4's SLB new-energy rebrand) — the incumbents trying to own a piece of their own disruption.

The Smil counterweight (both sides, honestly). Energy transitions historically take decades, and hydrocarbons are still ~80% of primary energy — so "the end of oil" is a direction, not a date. The node teaches the mechanism and the tension, and deliberately does not issue a peak-demand verdict.

The AI cross-current (the live twist). Just as the transition points demand down, AI and data-center electricity demand is pushing power demand up, reviving gas and nuclear as bridge fuels — the thread that ties this node to sector-information-technology.z5.2 (the two reinvention nodes, now cross-linked both ways) and to that module's data-center power story.

The market shadow. Energy's shrinking index weight (★G137/★G138/★G140) — from mid-teens percent of the S&P decades ago to low single digits — is the transition already partly priced into the sector's footprint (and the reason the "capital discipline" regime in Z3.2/Z4.1 exists: why drill for growth the market may not want?).

connects_to.note: the stranded-asset tension — reserves as maybe-not-an-asset
